Author: @senorsnout4417
Text:
I would argue a deeper issue with EV adoption is also maintenance. Especially in more rural areas, it can be hard to find not only EV charging stations, but also someone to help you fix an issue if something goes wrong with your car. If an ICV breaks down, a person *can* diagnose and fix the issue themselves, or take it to a local mechanic. If an EV breaks down, you can pretty much only take it to a dealership. And if you're far from one...good luck, you're fucked.
Like, the nearest traditional mechanic where I live is about 5 minutes away. The nearest dealership is closer to 40.

Author: @Mike-yr1sm
Text:
I understand the topic is "mass adoption", but a hugely built out DC fast-charging infrastructure seems like a waste.
Spamming them around interstates makes sense. 
Besides that, it seems encouraging street/apartment/business charging at level 2 would go much further. Or, at least, level 1.
Most people don't drive that far on most days.
I have owned an EV in a cold weather zone for over 3 years now. Two of the three winters I lived off of level 1 charging and managed. It does take more intentionality during the winter to avoid range anxiety, but I have never been below 50 miles of range.
I do now have level 2, and it is amazingly convenient; however, more than I absolutely need.

Author: @Steve-cl7hr
Text:
Rewatching this 4 years later, how things have changed!  The US moved from CCS to Tesla’s NACS - not by government mandate, but by the auto industry recognizing the superior NACS standard was the way to go.  

Otherwise, the video was prescient.  Sure, there is still a lot of consumer reluctance over EVs.  Totally justifiable by use case and initial purchase price without a government subsidy.  I think some of that reluctance will evaporate now that consumers don’t feel pushed by an EV mandate.  It will be interesting to see where we will be 4 years from now.
Cleaned_Text: Rewatching this 4 years later, how things have changed! The US moved from CCS to Tesla’s NACS - not by government mandate, but by the auto industry recognizing the superior NACS standard was the way to go. Otherwise, the video was prescient. Sure, there is still a lot of consumer reluctance over EVs. Totally justifiable by use case and initial purchase price without a government subsidy. I think some of that reluctance will evaporate now that consumers don’t feel pushed by an EV mandate. It will be interesting to see where we will be 4 years from now.

Author: @mr88cet
Text:
I’ve watched this video many times over the years since it was first published.  Quite a few things have improved since then:

First, EV manufacturers, for the North-American market, are standardizing on the Tesla (AKA “NACS”) charging connector.  By mid-2025, the majority of EVs will be able to charge at Tesla Superchargers.

Second, charging speeds are improving, partly because of 800V vehicles like the Ioniq 5, EV6, and Porsche Taycan.  Charging 20-80% usually doesn’t take much more time than going to the bathroom and eating a quick snack.

Also, there are a lot more fast-charging stations to go around now.  In general, road tripping is not a big issue for most people at least.
Cleaned_Text: I’ve watched this video many times over the years since it was first published. Quite a few things have improved since then: First, EV manufacturers, for the North-American market, are standardizing on the Tesla (AKA “NACS”) charging connector. By mid-2025, the majority of EVs will be able to charge at Tesla Superchargers. Second, charging speeds are improving, partly because of 800V vehicles like the Ioniq 5, EV6, and Porsche Taycan. Charging 20-80% usually doesn’t take much more time than going to the bathroom and eating a quick snack. Also, there are a lot more fast-charging stations to go around now. In general, road tripping is not a big issue for most people at least.

Text:
Wendover productions would do well to get someone with at least minimal electric knowledge to edit their videos. 
1) There is an overarching implecation that the AC power system is the problem because charging needs DC. All electronic equipment, all electroplqating, arc lighting, most heavy motors in steel rolling plants (massive DC amps), aluminum production (more nassive DC), etc. use DC and have for more than 20 years. The early rectifiers were tubes for high voltages of various types, then in the 1960's we started to see solid state rectifiers. India is doing a long line ultra high voltage DC power transfer that reuiires power plant levels (think thousands of MW) of power to be rectified to DC at the tens of million volt levels (thousands of amps) , and then turned back into AC at the other end, the reduced lossed making up for the increased one in the conversion. CHANGING AC TO DC IS SIMPLE AND EASY. It is not in any way the roadblock to the battery charging process except it has to be designed.  That has been done.  Someone is biased here. 
2) The Tesla charger (5:54) does not use and i quote "power iverter" that converts DC to AC,  it needs a rectifier(Ad to DC).
3)  a "standard wall outlet" in the US comes in two varieties, 15 amp and 20 amp. Anything else is a special outlet, usually a 230 volt. There are few homes with 3 phase power.  A 15 amp circuit is 15 times 120 volts ( 1,800 watts - 1.8 KW) and 20 Amp i(20 times 120 or 2,400 watts, 2.4 KW_ and even one of the largest seen in the home is one for a kitchen range, usually 50 amps or less at 240 volts - 50 times 240 is 12,000 watts or 12 KW.  An average EV needs 0.3 kwh per mile, think of it, 3.3 miles per KWH.  Suppose the driver had a 40 mile round trip commute with no other driving.  He needa 40 times 0.3 KWH per day, or 12 KWH.  If the bettery will handle the charging rate, (most lilely not the full amount) he needs 1 hour on the range receptacle, 5 hours on the 20 amp 120 volt and 6.7 hours on the 15 amp circuit, a viable number.  But if the commute is 80 miles with some other driving, the range goes to two hours and the other two go to 10 hours or 13 hours. The car must be plugged in from 8 pm till 6 AM. At my rate, (0.19 cents per KWH) in CA it is 30 cents.  Neglecting any inefficienty the 70 kwh would translate to 70 times 21 or 1470 KWH, The average home use in CA is 640 a month. The EV would increase the bill from about 200 dollars to over 700 dollars and nearly quaduple the grid load if all cars became EV.s.  CA cannot handle the current load and in fact PA, one in better condition could not even bear one half of this. 

4)  Inverter, inverter... 
5) EV;s smoke and mirrors.

Author: @Deadlious
Text:
The Problem is partially about the charging stations. Yes, we need more. However, even if there is a charging station on each kilometer, I would not buy an EV with the current technology level, especially a Tesla. Having a huge screen in the middle with no other significant control, is a safety risk I'm not willing to take. And while I see you say there are are technologies like lane assist and autopilot, they are not good enough if the conditions are not ideal - e.g. snowy road, obstacles and missing signs or bad markings. In addition to that, I would only buy a car that can make 1000km in 10hours. If it can't do that range in that amount of time, no thanks.

Back to the problem - on one hand there are the missing stations, on the other hand there is the battery technology, that doesn't allow storage of larger amounts of energy per unit of mass and the time constraints for recharging. The cars might be good enough for city driving in metropolitan areas, but not everyone can purchase and support 2-3 cars, and have one for city, one for country and one for off-road or a van to fit your entire family.

Not to mention ecological concerns about used battery packs or how the rare materials are obtained, cause this is a whole other story of why some people still avoid them.

Author: @richardjohnson4052
Text:
my main problem is battery range.  
I can fill my KIA, drive 400 miles, pull into a gas station and in five minutes I am back on the road.  I do NOT want to look for a charging station that fits MY car and sit there for a few hours while it changes, hours I can better us driving to my destination.  And how many stations are on an Indian Reservation in Arizona or a small town in the desert or a lake marina?
So If I do the 50% charge idea, I cannot drive from Tucson to Phoenix on one charge.  This means that when my gf needs me to take her to Sky Harbor to catch a flight to Illinois for her father who is in the Hospital, I had better have my battery fully charged and even then I cannot reach Phoenix on that charge because that airport is 120 miles away and When I arrive, I will sit in the lot for a few hours waiting to charge my vehicle while each person plugs their car in and takes off for an hour while their car is charged and parked, gone while the car IS fully charged but they do not know this.  At a shell station, the driver will NOT have a meal while his tank is filling.  

I am willing to sit #5 in line at a gas station because I know that each car will remain at the pump for 5-minutes, 10 if it is a truck with an extra tank.  Total wait time is 20 minutes.   If the same happens at a charging station, If I am #5, my wait time has jumped to 3-4 HOURS!  all the time I am draining my battery.

my secondary problem is the cost of the battery.
My daughter had a hybrid and had to sell it because when the main battery died, she could not afford to replace it.  So now she drives a normal car.


As an in-town car, a EV is fine IF I get into the habit of plugging it in EVERY time I go home or go to a store.  BUT, the first time I offload the melting ice cream and other groceries  from the store and forget to plug the car in, I will be late for work the next am.

Author: @danc.5859
Text: Great video, as your videos always are. But as an owner of a Tesla as well, I do have to say that the biggest thing holding EVs back isn’t precisely the lack of an infrastructure. It’s more the consumers’ expectations of what they need for an infrastructure. When I first bought my EV, I thought that waiting a half hour or more to charge my vehicle would be a significant downside, though one I was willing to live with. However, in reality, I found that I actually don’t use high-speed chargers anywhere near as often as I thought I was going to. The real thing that makes electric vehicle charging much more convenient than most people realize is when you can charge it from home. Even if it takes hours to do so, having the ability to charge your car overnight is a complete game changer. 95% of the time or more, my car has more than enough power to do the driving I need to do for the day just based on what it filled up to overnight. The fact that it takes hours to charge from home is irrelevant, since I’m not inconvenienced in any way while it is happening - I’m usually asleep. It takes me 5 seconds to plug it in, and that’s far more convenient than going to a gas station every few days, even if the gas station only takes five or 10 minutes to get to and refill your vehicle. Sure, more charging stations and compatibility will help greatly to solve the charging problem when someone is on a “road trip” (and for context, gas station pumps cost tens of thousands of dollars too). But in reality, provided, of course that you live somewhere where you can plug in your electrical vehicle from home, the infrastructure required to support electric vehicles on the road is actually far less than that required to support refueling gasoline vehicles, considering that gasoline vehicles are never refilled from home.  For most people, at least those with the ability to charge from home, they just don’t drive far enough on a daily basis where they need high speed public chargers at all most of the time. I would be curious to know what proportion of driving comes from home charging vs public chargers, but I know it’s far higher than most people expect. As for myself, I know that I use public chargers less than 5 percent of the time, and I’m sure I’m not the exception. The bottom line is that EVs are already a perfectly acceptable option for most people, but they are held back by preconceived notions of what they need based on what they were used to from before, without realizing how those needs will change with newer technology.

Author: @anoopnair6927
Text: The main issue for me is surely "charging". The manual for affordable fast charging EV says "DO NOT CHARGE, DURING A THUNDERSTORM OR LIGHTNING". That is a real issue, Where I live in India thunderstorms and lightning are an any-time occurrence, The current petrol or diesel vehicles does not get affected by rain lightning or thunderstorms, as we know it from experience, we just need to be careful about water logged roads and reduced visibility during a rain. which are abundant throughout our tropical climate. Imagine charging an EV at night on the side of a road, with your kids and family accompanying on a drive and during the rains which surely makes the whole sky light up with lightning streams, is beyond acceptable risk. The very thought sends shivers.  And to add up to that I am yet to see a charging station on roadside with a proper shelter for occupants to wait. Horrible is one word to describe the situation. I hope, someday,  EV batteries can be swapped at so-called charging stations, so we can just hand over the empty battery and get a refill, Just like our LPG cylinders. That may not happen because, since every manufaturer has different specs for battery packs, Unlike unleaded fuel or Diesel which every car or other vehicle can simply fill in.

Author: @SeauxNOLALady
Text: The range anxiety is a problem for people who haven’t experienced driving an electric vehicle before. I suggest renting one for a week, but only if you live in an urban area where there are charging options available. If someone lives in a very rural or isolated area, electric vehicles can be more of a hindrance than a benefit. I live near multiple charging stations. My boyfriend has a Tesla Model S Plaid and he had a level two charger put in his garage. So he plugs his car in every night when the electricity is cheapest but if he needs to fully charge his car quickly he goes to the supercharger. He can charge it from nearly zero to 80% (which is the highest battery level you need and it will only slow charge the remaining twenty percent anyway) in a little over an hour. The supercharger is outside of a Winn Dixie so we have plugged in and then went grocery shopping while it charges. We also have a plug in hybrid vehicle which we use for any long distance drive. Nothing beats being able to just gas up and get back on the road immediately though…

Author: @154Kilroy
Text:
When electric cars first starting becoming popular I was all for it. The instant torque was a positive for me. But now the government and people in general are pushing for them so hard I no longer have an interest. Its become a political statement to buy one. And I just have no want for that. 
 But not only that, I live in Minnesota. The temperatures varies by over 100f here between the seasons and a 31 minute charge time would be completely unacceptable. It would be longer because of the cold anyways. But standing outside when its -35 out and the wind is blowIng SUCKS. 5 minutes is a long time. Let alone 30. Until they get it down to 5 min or less charging time, its impractical for someone like me. And we're a long ways off from that too.
 I also like roadtrips. I just brought my 2006 Acura TL with 226k miles (when I started) on a 4,500 miles roadtrip from Minneapolis to Phoenix and back while taking the scenic route. That would quite literally be impossible in any EV because of the reasons discussed in this video. And adding on hours of charging time would have made the 2 weeks I took off from work not really viable either. As it was I didnt get to see as much as I wanted. There wouldve been basically no time if I had to waste so much time at charging stations. Thats not a good time on vacation. 
 I'd buy a hydrogen car if the infrastructure was there for that, though.

Author: @Theegreygaming
Text: I require a pickup so that's the big hold back or me. while EV cars are approaching the cost and range barriers, EV pickups still have a long way to go. also I live in a region with extremely sparse charging stations so while I could drive my car around town just fine, I can't ever leave my geographic region because my car would die before I reached the first public charging station. also there's the whole cost of a new battery and if a career in IT has taught me anything... those batteries in your car will only have 2-3 good years before you start to experience noticeable reduction in range and within 5 years you'll need a new battery... the single highest cost of an EV. so every 5 years you get to dump another $10-15K on a new battery and that's only if the rapidly approaching battery crunch is met with a battery production boom... which it won't be because the majority will be earmarked for new production, rather than being made available for service stations.

Author: @ryuukeisscifiproductions1818
Text:
2 is not going to work because of the different electrical standards in the world. Much of the world uses 230/400 volt three phase mains power supply, which easily allows EV's in those countries to use three phase 400 volt onboard chargers that charge much faster than American 240 volt chargers. where as American chargers, because three phase power is so rare in residential situations, they instead rely on 120 and 240 volt onboard chargers, which cannot also be made compatible with 400 volt three phase very easily. To add further problems, when commercial EV's start coming out in numbers, the best way to charge them in reasonable times is to add 208 and 480 volt three phase compatibility, or 600 volt for canadians.

Now a 230/400 volt EV charger can charge off an American 240 volt supply, they do have single phase charging compatibility, but those chargers are not compatible with 120, 208, 277 or 480 volt charging, so harmonizing every EV charger to the 230/400 volt standard means the high capacity 400 volt charging will never be used on American vehicles, making it a waste, and eliminating 120 volt chargers doesnt work for those with only access to 120 volt charging. Conversely going with American 120/240 volt charging is a problem for 230/400 volt countries since they cant utilize their greater mains supply voltage for faster charging, and putting a high capacity single phase charger running on just one leg of a three phase transformer is really not good for the transformer.

And then you have japan with their 100 volt mains.

In short you cant really harmonize charging connectors without harmonizing the worlds power grid, which is not an easy solution.

Author: @jeremiahpuckett5836
Text:
Great video, but there are some problems you didn’t mention.

First, most of us don’t want to stop for 30 minutes to charge every 100 miles.  Especially with kids OMG!  Americans are road trip people.  We have no problems driving halfway across the state to visit a friend, or simply going to a niche restaurant.  We also love taking toys to the mountains or lakes.  Dirt bikes, ATVs, jet skis, snowmobiles, and campers.  We tow.  The Rivian and other tow-capable vehicles can barely tow 100 miles on a full charge, so using the idea of charging to 50% would have us spending more time charging than driving… literally.  F that!

Another problem is we don’t want base models.  Sure.  There are base model EVs under $32k but as soon as you want dual motor or a nicer trim the costs jump up.  We love our bells and whistles we now have with our mid and upper-trim ICE vehicles.  Premium sound, leather seats, heated steering wheels.  And, let’s talk about size.  We want an SUV, not a compact car.  So, the Leaf and Bolt are NEVER going to be what we want en masse.  EV SUVs are nowhere near the $32k price.  You’re talking closer to $60k and even upward of $80k.

Personally, I like the idea of a PHEV.  Sadly, even these are falling short.  Most have a range around 30 miles before they start using gas.  My 30-minute commute is 34 miles round trip, but then that’s it.  It’s an overnight charge in my garage to get another 30 miles of electric for tomorrow .  So, if I decide after work to go shopping, bowling, out to eat, etc my PHEV is an ICE vehicle.  So, what’s the point?

One other thing that is a roadblock is the system of incentive.  Right now, I can expect as much as $15k total in state and federal tax credits.  But if I buy the vehicle now, in February, I won’t see that benefit until NEXT YEAR.  So, I have to get a loan for that $60k Mach-E without that $15k incentive.  $600-700 monthly payment.  The government should have worked something out with banks that incentives could go straight to the bank or loan.  Here’s what that would mean.

Let’s assume my vehicle is a $20k trade-in.  The Mach-E is $55k out the door and I’d qualify for $15k in tax incentives.  I could do quick math as to what the car is really going to cost me.  $55k - $20k - $15k.  Ooh!  Only $20k for a brand new EV.  Nope, I can’t calculate the loan with the incentive as a down payment.  So, my loan isn’t $20k which I could do.  My loan and monthly payment is nearly double, at $35k.  So, I could refinance the loan after I get the incentives, but interest rates on used cars are higher than new cars.  Some banks will re-amortize the loan payment amount if you send them a big check toward the loan amount.  Some.  The government should have given the incentive directly to the dealership instead of to the tax return.  But, we all know what government interference does.  We’ve seen this with solar panel systems, college, and health care. As soon as the government says we will give xx dollars, the cost magically goes up by xx dollars.

17 states have passed the Communist California’s CARB law.  We only have 10 more years of ICE vehicles on dealer lots.  The EVs are being forced upon us.  Tech isn’t really getting that much better.  Batteries are getting bigger, not much denser.  Batteries might be getting cheaper,  but we aren’t seeing that and supposedly car manufacturers are losing money on EVs.  Supposedly.  Charging isn’t really getting faster.  We are just told to charge halfway and twice as often to make it appear it’s getting faster.

Author: @natehill8069
Text: With current (npi) battery technology, you dont charge to 100% on trips. You typically run down to 10-15% and then charge up to 60-70%. The first 10% and last ~30% of capacity charge much slower; so you get there faster by more, shorter charging stops; every 2-3 hours or so.

Author: @ninjawaiter
Text:
Pretty crap.  This video doesn't even touch on the actual charging problems.  Getting the charging time down to 30 minutes MIGHT satisfy enough consumers, but only IF they can actually start charging immediately when they arrive at the station.  Currently medium-sized gas stations have about a dozen pumps, which means they can fuel about a dozen cars at once.  Each car takes less than 5 minutes to fuel, on average.  Meaning that such a station could fuel about 144 cars an hour.  In order to charge those same 144 cars at 30 minutes per car, you would need not 12 pumps, but 72 individual charging stations.  Imagine how much more real estate that's going to require.  Where will you put that?  Many roads simply don't have the space.  One solution is to spread out the chargers.  Instead of 72 chargers in one place, you put 2 chargers here and 2 chargers there, all along the major routes.  But that  creates more problems than it solves, for lots of reasons.  Firstly, when people stop, they need to do other things.  They need to use the bathroom, for one.  Which means you'd need bathrooms at every place there are chargers.  And people to clean those bathrooms, and cleaning supplies, and extra toilet paper and paper towels, not to mention all the infrastructure to support the facilities.  It's all ridiculously impractical, so we're 100% back to a ton of charging stations in one location.  Which leads us into the next problem.  Trust me, this whole thing gets much, much worse.

It's easy to occupy people for 5 minutes while they fuel up a gas vehicle.  That's actually less time than is typically needed to stretch one's legs, urinate, and maybe buy a drink and snack.  But when you stretch that stop time out to 30 minutes, not only are you making people's entire transit time longer, but you have to give them something to DO while they wait.  People hate to be bored, and they're going to HATE the experience of waiting for their vehicle to charge.  This isn't insurmountable, but whatever you're going to design to entertain people is going to require even more space at the charging location.  But hey, we haven't even gotten to the REAL problems with this whole EV charging thing.

I'm not going to quote numbers, because I don't have them handy.  But those fast-charging stations, they take a TON of power.  Like, they practically need their own power sub-station to operate.  In order to pump that much electricity into a battery that quickly, they use something like the equivalent of the power needed for an entire neighborhood.  Put a whole bunch of them together, and you're talking about enough power to supply a city.  You need the infrastructure to actually get all that power to the charging stations, so that's a major deal.  You also have to deal with the problems of balancing load on the power source, since the difference between 0 cars charging and 25 cars charging at once is like an entire city turning on their AC at the same time.  If you're not familiar with how these things have to be actively managed by power plants, look it up.  When you're talking about drawing that much power per station with that many stations, it's a gigantic nightmare in the making.  But that's not as much of a problem as actually getting all that power out to the locations where it'll be needed.  That will require major power substations, or more likely building actual individual power plants for each of these charging station blocks.  Because the current grids don't have that much power to spare.  In order to provide the kind of power needed to fast charge all these EVs, we'd have to MASSIVELY increase the electricity we produce.  And if you're in this for the green energy revolution, I can only laugh in your face.  Because they only possible way we could do that is to build an absolute crap-ton of coal-fired plants, or to go all-in on nuclear like we're trying to build the world of the Fallout games.  I don't personally mind either option, but the people pushing for EVs sure will.

And we haven't even touched on temperature, and the fact that in cold weather it doesn't matter how much power you're pumping out, it's STILL going to take 3-5 hours to charge your EV, only for it to go 50 miles and need another 3-5 hour charge.  Because physics and electricity and batteries.  And that's not even ADDRESSING extreme cold, where your battery just literally won't charge, and you won't be able to drive again until the temperatures warm in, possibly in a couple weeks.  Good luck with that!

The point of all this is that EVs are a bad joke, the taxpayers who are paying for them via subsidies and other government programs (that's you!) are the punchline, and anyone telling you differently is either an idiot, is profiting from the scam, or has an ulterior motive (like eliminating personal vehicular transportation entirely) and is simply lying and hiding behind the EV scam.

Ha!  All that and I didn't even touch on how the overwhelming majority of the electricity we're using currently comes from fossil fuels, so your EV might as well be powered by coal.  All you've done is add extra steps.

Author: @limburgercheese1234
Text: I'm on my second EV with a stated range of about 250 miles. It's great for daily commuting since I'm fortunate to charge at home. I've taken it on a few longer trips and the vehicle gets from point A to B but not nearly as conveniently as a gas car. Once you are on the road the calculating begins between range and the next charging station. Let's say I want to drive 200 miles before I charge but the available stations are at 100 miles and 250 miles. This is common once you leave a city center. I have no choice but to charge at the first one. Normal practice is to charge the vehicle to 80% capacity, (200). It's another 150 miles to the next station so I'm stopping there too. The real range of my 250 mile vehicle becomes around 125 miles per charging stop and at least 30 minutes added to my commute time every time I stop. Add some cold or hot weather and steep grades and my car may not even make it at all. I've never gotten stuck but have cut it very close a couple times. My next car will be a plug-in hybrid.

Author: @dpsamu2000
Text:
The challenge with EVs is perception. People think they need a vehicle with the numbers cited. But those numbers are totally arbitrary. They are not based on actual typical vehicle use. Actual average daily range is 25 to 50 miles. Considering that a second vehicle for most daily use can be delivered for $5000 or less. A single seat EV car can be delivered for $2000. EV bikes are $800 to $1500. With a few hundred dollars of accessories for grocery cargo they can do most of what we do with a car. Protection from weather is the only thing more we need from cars. Making a much cheaper EV second car to fill those basic needs will be what will bring the tipping point. EV bike sales, and use is skyrocketing. It is very useful  for the poor. By having the range to access the economies of scale in large supermarkets it reduces the cost of living for the poor. The reduction of cost of living offsets the cost of the vehicles.

It's the application of EV technology to serve the market in the most needed way that will change perceptions, and show it to be acceptable.

Author: @andrewwatson5360
Text:
The reason governments are not getting involved to any real extent in providing infrastructure is that to reach a viable level for full fleet adoption of battery electric vehicles will be impossibly expensive not just for the charging stations but more importantly for the reticulation of electricity to power them. 
BEVs are at best an interim transport system with underdeveloped technology and numerous ongoing problems (including fire safety) that have not been adequately addressed.
Unless these issues are solved in the near future it is economics that will kill off their general adoption and confine their use to denser population zones where they can provide practical and cleaner transportation in a compact form. 
Other developing technologies combined with existing high tech power sources will provide overall vehicle lifetime cleaner and more economic transport in the near future.

Author: @KarlUppianoKarlU
Text: Whether we have an AC or DC power grid makes no difference to vehicle charging. It's just that residential power distribution hasn't been optimized for the kinds of loads that fast vehicle charging requires. And maybe it should never be. If we replaced every gasoline station with a charging station, that would be no problem from a power grid standpoint. Most gasoline stations are in the vicinity of industrial three phase power. I still think people should consider battery swapping, instead of in-vehicle charging. With the appropriate standardization, batteries could be charged, owned and maintained by charging stations. This would move the cost and responsibility of battery ownership from the vehicle owners to the charging stations. Barcodes or QR codes could be used to track batteries in central databases, and ensure that the vehicle owners wouldn't be saddled with defective batteries, and would track the usage, condition, billing and so on.

Author: @charlesbolin7207
Text:
I don't think the government should be offering any business a financial incentive to produce EVs or subsidize them in any way. The incentive to cut costs and lower prices to an affordable level goes out the window when a manufacturer can tell customers not to worry about the price because the government is picking up part of the tab.

When government started subsidizing college educations most colleges increased amenities and programs. They started requiring more credits to graduate citing that classes not required in a career field create "well rounded students". Colleges stopped competing over price and started competing over amenities and look what it's created, overwhelming debt with people looking for the government to just forgive it.

We are going that way with electric cars. Manufacturers are looking at self driving technology, infotainment centers, built in GPS, Lane Assist, Park Assist, Rapid Charging and everything but cost all because of the philosophy of "don't worry about it, the government wants people to have them so bad they will subsidize them, focus on amenities and don't worry about the cost".

Get rid of every government subsidy and tell the EV makers "No more tax breaks, No more subsidies, EVs will be charged road tax when charging, make them affordable for everyone WITHOUT INDEFINITE FINANCING or go out of business because you can't compete with traditional gas and diesel auto makers", and watch the price of EVs go down. I guarantee you if every government tax incentive or subsidy was removed from EV's the Tesla Model 3 would drop in price by $10k, In fact I bet each one probably doesn't cost much more than $15k to manufacture.

Text: Thanks for the video. The thing I wonder about though is what happens once we reach widespread adoption of EVs. I mean those charging stations use a ton of electricity, enough to power a number of average households. That's going to demand enormous amounts of electricity from the grid. So where are we going to get all this raw electricity? I mean the last time I checked nearly every industrialized country except China is getting rid of their of their nuclear power plants and phasing out coal fired power stations. I mean the electrical grid's in every country where EVs are highly adopted is going to have to massively expand yet I don't hear anyone explaining how that's going to happen. China might be able to handle a rapid increase EVs since they are expanding their electrical grid but everyone else in the developed world are such Anti carbon Purists that they don't seem to be doing very much to boost the grid's carrying capacity if anything countries like Germany that are getting rid of all their nuclear power plants and just going "pure green" aren''t going to be able to provide the raw power needed to charge all these EVs. Anytime I see this issue come up it immediately gets swept under the rug by all these Green People who just say don't worry about it technology will solve everything. But that's not really an answer. Unless there's a MAJOR breakthrough in fusion power and I mean commercially viable fusion for powering electrical grids every nation on Earth is going to need to double or tipple their electrical power generating capacity and your NOT going to do that with just solar panels and wind turbines. So unless we are going to build a bunch of coal and natural gas stations were not going to have the juice to do this which means rationing and rolling blackouts. Personally I don't relish the idea of being like South Africia which has a severe shortage of power generating stations and is constantly experiencing brown outs and black outs. Is that the future of EVs having your power go out every other day?   Or are we just going to build a bunch of coal and oil power stations and just ignore the fact that were burning even more fossil fuels while simulatiously claiming we are winning the fight against climate change? Or is the World Economic Forum just going to push for the end of private vehicle ownership and the only way we are going to move around is on an electric bus? Personally I don't like the idea of not having my own private transportation and having to hail a taxi or a bus to go anywhere.      It just seems like the control freaks who are pushing this new greener EV future have no concrete plans on how our infrastructure and utilities are going to be upgraded and reconfigured to do all this. My worry is that the politicians that are pushing this climate change agenda don't have a plan and are just going to ban everything we use today without thinking things through and leave us with a bridge to no where. Like ban all combustion vehicles without scaling up our grid enough so we keep overloading the grid or get rid of all our nuclear reactors, scrap our coal powered stations and try to run our entire grid off a few solar panels and windfarms and force everyone to ration their energy consumption.                       Until someone explains how we are going to generate more electricity in a reliable way I am very skeptical this is going to be a smooth energy transition but instead is likely to be a very awkward and disastrous project that ultimately fails.

Author: @dogman2387
Text: A range extended EV isn't even considered in this video.  Take an EV with 300-400 miles range and replace 3/4 of the battery pack with a gas engine range extender. The resulting 75-100 mile EV range would cost less and cover around 95% of your miles on electricity just by charging at home. No need to wait for a new infrastructure of fast chargers. Interestingly, Elon Musk proposed this idea. Just Google "tesla song remains same". Here's a quote: "Tesla will likely provide both pure electric and range extended electric drive options in the future. We refer to the latter as a REEV (Range Extended Electric Vehicle) to distinguish it from "hybrids," which are really just gasoline engine cars with a small electric motor and tiny battery. The REEV battery in our scenario would fully cover the range needs for reasonable daily usage, but there would be an onboard generator for the occasional long trip."

Author: @Snubbs
Text:
I get this video's a little old at this point, but why doesn't it cover home charging? Most people put so few miles on their cars per day that an overnight plug-in on a low-range level 2 charger can fully replenish what was lost. This makes the effective charge time basically nil, and also invalidates a lot of the range concerns for basically everyone except travelling salespeople and road trip fanatics. 

I'm obviously a sample size of one, but the main reason I haven't considered buying an EV is longevity concerns and upfront costs. the cheapest EV near me with less than 300,000km on it is a 2014 nissan leaf for $15,000. I know that it'll be cheaper to own in the long run compared to my current car, but that's a crossover that happens at the 6+ year mark, and it assumes that the then 15-year-old car won't need any emergency repairs or replacements of its expensive, non-user-serviceable parts.

Author: @J4Zonian
Text:
Solutions: 
Federally imposed charger standards & mandated, funded charger building. 

France requires solar panels over parking lots. The US needs to, along with attached chargers. It also needs to require or irresistibly incentivize solar electrification & charging for commercial fleets, shopping & employment places. 

Not just tax deductions but breaks on sales tax, plus direct subsidies & other assistance for EVs, like the ones that have given Norway its world-leading 90% EV market share. Carpool lane access, lower tolls on roads, bridges, ferries. Like all taxes & fees, these should be progressive wherever possible.

Author: @red58fury
Text: I road-trip a lot. Have a 2022 Explorer and it already has 35k miles on it. It’s also my daily driver at the moment since I had to sell my other gas car. So for me to even think about buying an EV I would have to keep a gas car as well. I would charge the EV overnight on a 120 volt outlet and use it as a daily driver to and from work and keep the explorer as the family hauler and road trip car. I would only purchase an EV if I could charge at home. If I lived in an apartment with no EV chargers and had to drive somewhere and wait 30 min to 1 hour to fast charge there’s no way I’d purchase an EV. Plus constantly super charging a battery can degrade the battery rapidly. Now every house won’t be able to support a level 2 charger safely. So if you are able to install 1 at your home it could cost thousands to get your home up to code to allow a 240v charger.

Text:
If we do not commit an incredible amount of money on electrical power generation, distribution and delivery infrastructure, an EV in every garage is never going to be feasible. Oh, and an inverter converts DC power to AC power.

Author: @MikeInPlano
Text:
This was posted 2 years ago, and the same flawed conclusions are still flawed, while the same EV shortcoming are yet to be overcome, specifically because of the federal government's meddling in the free market.
I disagree with the premise the current EV range is acceptable, and also the concept presented of charging to 50% every 100 miles.  On a long trip I refuse to stop for 30 minutes every 300 miles, and certainly not for 15 minutes every 100 miles.  Yes, I have to stop to refuel my internal combustion engine vehicle every 300 miles, but that's a 5-10 minute stop before I'm back on the road.  That additional 20 minutes for an EV adds of to a lot of extra time to my trip, and I refuse to accept that.  The solution is either 10 minute recharge time (preferably 5 minutes) to 100% (not 75%), or a fast battery swap that takes 10 minutes or less.  Neither of these is going to happen in the next 10 years, so count me out.  
And then there's the power grid problem. I live in north Texas, and over the past several weeks the string of 105+ degree days has maxed the power grid.  We're being asked to voluntarily cut back electricity consumption.  What if all of us (or even just 50% of us) were driving EV's?  
And I heartily disagree with the conclusion that it's the federal government's job to create the necessary charging infrastructure, something borne out by the fact that none of the flaws in this video have been resolved, or even seen significant progress.  
EV manufacturer's know it's in their best interests to come up with a standard for charger ports so they can all take advantage of any fast-charging station across the country, rather than each of them investing $250,000 or more for thousands of stations.  EV's have become a microcosm of all that's wrong with a government dictating to manufacturers and consumers.  Left to them, producers and consumers would soon enough sort out these EV issues in due time, and at much less expense for all.

I disagree with the premise the current EV range is acceptable, and also the concept presented of charging to 50% every 100 miles. On a long trip I refuse to stop for 30 minutes every 300 miles, and certainly not for 15 minutes every 100 miles. Yes, I have to stop to refuel my internal combustion engine vehicle every 300 miles, but that's a 5-10 minute stop before I'm back on the road. That additional 20 minutes for an EV adds of to a lot of extra time to my trip, and I refuse to accept that. The solution is either 10 minute recharge time (preferably 5 minutes) to 100% (not 75%), or a fast battery swap that takes 10 minutes or less. Neither of these is going to happen in the next 10 years, so count me out. And then there's the power grid problem. I live in north Texas, and over the past several weeks the string of 105+ degree days has maxed the power grid. We're being asked to voluntarily cut back electricity consumption. What if all of us (or even just 50% of us) were driving EV's? And I heartily disagree with the conclusion that it's the federal government's job to create the necessary charging infrastructure, something borne out by the fact that none of the flaws in this video have been resolved, or even seen significant progress. EV manufacturer's know it's in their best interests to come up with a standard for charger ports so they can all take advantage of any fast-charging station across the country, rather than each of them investing $250,000 or more for thousands of stations. EV's have become a microcosm of all that's wrong with a government dictating to manufacturers and consumers. Left to them, producers and consumers would soon enough sort out these EV issues in due time, and at much less expense for all.

Author: @DubstepsHub
Text:
This is a late response but it's important to note that the charging speed problem can, in fact, be looked at as a range problem. Since EVs can very quickly charge the first 50% of a battery, all you need to do is provide drivers with a battery that has more range than the magic number of 291 - an EV that has a range of 500 miles that can charge to 58% in 30 minutes is enough to provide both the range and charging speed a user might want, and if you happen to have more time (like if you're charging overnight or happen to be stopping for longer) then you have the added benefit of more capacity to work with.

Of course, the ideal solution is to improve on both fronts, but as EVs start to release with ranges in excess of 400 miles (I've even seen one with a range of 516mi, though it sports a six figure price tag) I feel that the charging issue is rapidly vanishing.

Author: @LarryMcLarren
Text:
Ok, that was a good video. Eventhough I am not certain about electric vehicles overtaking combustion engines in all of traffic, they will definitely play an important role.

One thing you did not mention here considering the infrastructure is yet another problem, where I will talk especially from a German perspective as I am from Germany: It's not only about where you have the DC chargers and whether you have enough of them or not, it is also about how much more electric energy will be used if fossile fuels would completely get replaced by electric energy. With that, the demand to produce and deliver electric energy would increase extremely, which has to happen with green energy from renewable energies or nuclear energy. If the electrical energy would be produced by combustion of coal or gas, it would go against the whole idea of why people want to switch to electric cars in the first place. Then, also, the power grids have to have the necessary capacity to transport that increase of energy through the cities, since most of that energy need will rise in cities where the demand for electricity is already highest without electric cars. Now, these infrastructures have to be sufficient as well.

The reason why I think, electric cars will not completely replace combustion cars is:
- There are already CO2 neutral fuels (synthetic fuels) in developement. Syn fuels can be transported very easily over huge distances where electrical energy cannot, what means that you can produce these fuels from green energy sources wherever you want on Earth and just transport them anywhere.
- Charging an electric car is still way longer with 31 minutes than fueling up a combustion car.
- The already mentioned thing about the needed electrical energy to supply all electric cars.
- The energy density of even improved batteries is still worse than that of any common fuel, such that electric cars are way heavier than combustion cars while having lower range.
- The needed ressouces to produce enough batteries for a world with only electric cars is enormous and Lithium will get harder and harder to be accessed while the process of getting it will be less and less environmentally friendly.

As I said, sure, electric cars will play an important role, but I think they will not be the only ones. I think combustion engines with e-fuels will play another important role.

Author: @JLPicard1648
Text:
My US state of Colorado has about 37.5% of consumer energy coming from coal and 26.9% coming from natural gas. Driving 250 miles in an electric car charged from the grid produces about 56 pounds of CO2 emissions, from the power plants generating the energy. 

Driving the same distance with my 40 mpg mid-sized sedan produces about 120 pounds of CO2 emissions. And it's cheaper and more convenient. 

For me, the threshold for EV's doesn't have much to do with the vehicle itself, but with the infrastructure and renewables utilization of the grid I'm using to charge the EV. If renewables made up 70% of the consumer grid production, for example, then charging a full battery would be cheaper and the emissions produced by charging would be cut in half. 

EV's produce more emissions in the production stage, vs gas cars, and fewer emissions in the usage stage vs gas cars.

But, in my opinion, it's not a worthwhile reduction YET, in the current year of 2023. It goes from 0.4 pounds of CO2 per mile to 0.25 pounds of CO2 per mile. Not worthwhile enough to justify the increased ownership and maintenance costs and the loss of convenience, when there are gas stations on every corner.

Author: @elusivehare
Text: If a consumer only drives a range of 50 miles a day which is lower than the average, they could recharge their car in a few hours with a common 15 amp charger or in even less time with a more powerful power source and charger. Overnight charging would recover even more while someone, for example, sleeps so recharging daily wouldn't even be needed, especially during the warmer months. With long distance traveling, going to charging stations outside of major cities would seem like the best option but I'm sure more charging stations will pop up as EVs become more common. There is also an issue with fast charging, topping off a charge becomes significantly slower so you would only need to charge up to 80 percent which still provides plenty of range. If someone is driving a distance of more than 150 miles to their job or has a rather small battery pack, they may be able to get permission to use a basic charger while at their job to recharge in a parking lot/area if it has some sort of outlet nearby. There are plenty of EV chargers already but as mentioned before, the likelihood that more EV stations will become available is very great. There are already over 50 thousand charging stations in the US and I'm sure that if someone had a cross country trip, they could choose a station or several that also have a restaurant attached or possibly one with a, for example, mcdonalds close by and plug their car in and lock their charger while they get food / eat. Just within 20 miles of my location, one that isn't even close to a major city, there are at least 3 EV stations with food places connected or within walking distance of that would fit within a 25 to 30 minute timeline to avoid any issues with taking up a space without actively charging. I don't see a need for the upset over EVs and not enough charging stations when most people can charge at home and even with more than 1 EV since you can alternate who uses the charger/charging area. I don't even have a closed in space parking area, my parking space is actually about 15 feet from my house but using a really long and less powerful charger from the house is still possible. I don't have an outside outlets but even then I still have windows and even in winter it's still easy to block out the gap of the window through something like weather stripping, foam, or tape. I hope that within a few years I'll be able to get an EV as I don't often go more than 10 miles from my house.

Author: @jamess1314
Text:
While I find this video somewhat interesting, it's not very relevant to me as an EV owner.  I've owned three EVs.  The first was a 2013 Chevy Volt (a PHEV).  While the video does point out that the Volt is not able to use a DC Fast Charger, it failed to mention that the Volt has over 300 miles of range using the gasoline engine.  My current Volt has a range in excess of 400 miles.  I currently own a 2017 Chevy Bolt and a 2018 Chevy Volt.  

I don't normally drive long distance.  I have occasionally taken trips that required the use of DC Fast Charging, but my normal driving is well within the range of my cars.  I charge at home, usually over night.  The last time I used a commercial charger was on an overnight trip staying at a hotel with destination chargers.  My point is that I rarely have to charge at a level 3 charger that would require a wait.  On the rare occasions that I've used a DC Fast Charger, I stop at a location that also offers shopping or restaurants.  I've only been to a gas station with my Volt 2 or 3 times in the last year.  So I've saved significant time waiting for my car to 'refuel'.

Every morning, I wake up with a range over over 250 miles on my Bolt and over 400 Miles on my Volt.

I know other EV owners with similar usage patterns as me.  And for those who do need to travel long distance, applications such as ABRP will plan the trip for you.  And one more thing, Tesla charger will be required to charge any EV by 2024.

Bottom line is that if you need a vehicle for routine long distance travel, an EV is probably not your best choice for now.  But if you need a vehicle for normal commuting to work or school or running errands, an EV might be a good choice.

Author: @brucefrykman8295
Text:
We all wait in short lines at the grocery store, gas stations, etc as well as when "getting serviced" in  government  lines.  This is so because it is not economically feasible to provision for unexpected peak demand.   

When charging times are 45 minutes or more  instead of the typical five minutes for a gas pump.  peak demand will inevitably produce charging wait times reaching into the hours and do so unpredictably.

With this in mind, a market based solution is available:  I envision concierge charge stations where charging stations wait times might be regulated by the price one is willing to pay in order to avoid them.  Time is more important to some people than others and at some times (like fleeing hurricanes and blizzards or catching  an important flight) they will arise even for those who normally have a lot of time to waste.   

In this situation, I see the price for  low wait time charge rising so as to allow the more desperate among us (medical conditions and the like) to pay the piper's price to get on one's way.  What's a $300 surcharge for a quick fill-up when lives are on the line?    

I also see the need for special charge stations reserved for emergency vehicles, legislators,  mayors, and other important government officials whose time is far more precious than ours,  as they could be held up when they should instead be meeting to enact important policy matters designed to further refine and regulate our behavior.

What say?

Author: @13minutestomidnight
Text:
Thankyou for making this important point. The charging infrastructure is a big issue that becomes more problematic the bigger the geography you have to cover. I've seen other video coverage of EV's, and generally they don't want to bring up the problems because the issues with global warming make EV adoption so important - for example, they show very careful routes between two very carefully-chosen distant US cities like that proves the infrastructure is ubiquitous. However, the problems aren't going away (in fact, Tesla is hurting its own market share), and governments need to contribute to help EV adoption.

 The big problem, however, is that when you consider the majority of the globe who drive cars or bikes, these people are too poor to buy EV's now (these are the people who are still using old petrol cars or need to use (old) bikes because they can't afford cars), and in these poor areas, the infrastructure is definitely not going to be catching up for decades at least; some of these areas include entire countries that are struggling just to provide very basic medical services. And the populations that are  using vehicles in these countries are rising too as industrialisation increases, even if the percentage of the population using vehicles is lower than the developed world (this isn't just in Africa and India, but across much of Asia too). 

So, what is the large-scale solution to help combat vehicle-contributions to global warming? Maybe massive investment in using electricity to produce long-chain hydrocarbons cheaply and efficiently, so electricity from renewables and nuclear can be used to replace fossil-fuels wholesale (at least for car fuel). If the price for the production process can be brought down lower than oil, this environmentally-friendly option would be able to completely undercut oil.

Author: @FireMarshallStev
Text:
You do not need to recreate the driving distance to the current gasoline infrastructure. You need to have the infrastructure to fast charge for people who drive to an empty battery far away (i.e. cross-country trips along the Interstate system).

Large proportions if not the majority of Americans live in detached single-family homes with off-street parking. There's your gas station for the EV. Vast vast vast majority of mileage per day will probably be below 50mi. Can easily top up your battery overnight with a standard home charger or drive for several days to empty and then charge overnight at home.

For everyone else that doesn't have access to their own charging space on their own property, again the vast majority of trips are not cross-country so they would only need to charge maybe once every week or two. Certainly a lot of "group parking spaces" such as employer parking lots or apartments/condos will build slow charging stations as an amenity for residents/employees. We already see this in a lot of places.

This still will leave behind a bunch of people without good access to the EV charging. So we still need a strong  public transit system (including intercity). Ban cars for all I care, but that's not realistic, so if EVs are going to replace ICEs then the infrastructure problem is not as insurmountable as this video makes it seem.

Author: @texanplayer7651
Text:
This is so dumb that people want cars to have over 290 miles of autonomy. Most people, most of the time, don't even drive that much in 3 days, so what good is that for them? 150 miles is more than enough to satisfy the demand of most, and if well organized, even long trips aren't a problem.
And charging time also isn't a problem when you know how to organize and prepare a trip, for example if you have a once-in-a-year 200 miles trip to drive you can plan a lunch break at a restaurant after 100 miles where you can fast recharge the vehicle for an hour that you would have spent anyways at that same restaurant, and then drive the rest of the 100 miles.
Sure, you loose the comfort of being more "spontaneous" and not needing to sit down for 10 minutes at a table to make plans, but is that "comfort" of saving 10 minutes of your time once in a year really worth paying hundreds of more dollars on gas each year than you would have on electricity? Especially since you really only need to plan this trip exactly once, and then never more, because you already know where to find a recharging station.
Cleaned_Text: This is so dumb that people want cars to have over 290 miles of autonomy. Most people, most of the time, don't even drive that much in 3 days, so what good is that for them? 150 miles is more than enough to satisfy the demand of most, and if well organized, even long trips aren't a problem. And charging time also isn't a problem when you know how to organize and prepare a trip, for example if you have a once-in-a-year 200 miles trip to drive you can plan a lunch break at a restaurant after 100 miles where you can fast recharge the vehicle for an hour that you would have spent anyways at that same restaurant, and then drive the rest of the 100 miles. Sure, you loose the comfort of being more "spontaneous" and not needing to sit down for 10 minutes at a table to make plans, but is that "comfort" of saving 10 minutes of your time once in a year really worth paying hundreds of more dollars on gas each year than you would have on electricity? Especially since you really only need to plan this trip exactly once, and then never more, because you already know where to find a recharging station.

Author: @kevinlemon3467
Text:
I don't think the issue is actually charging stations, because we're thinking of this with the mindset that we will fuel electric cars the same way that we fuel ICE vehicles. The issue is access to home charging. I own a Chevy Volt and I own my house. I charge it up at night and I never have to visit a charging station. The Volt also has an ICE engine in it as well, so while my range on electric is about 48 miles, I have as much actual range as I want. However, I almost never use the ICE engine. It turns on when it is really cold outside to heat up the battery and if I go on a trip over 50 miles, I'll use it, but I'd say 90% of my miles are electric. 
Still, it only has that 48 miles of electric power and I almost never go over that because I can charge it at home. If I can drive almost entirely electric on 48 miles of range, then most people could do it with 200-300. I don't even have a fast charger. I only have a Level 1 charger, which gets me about 4 miles per hour of charge, but I generally don't drive more than 40 miles per day, so it doesn't matter. 
My work also lets us plug into 110 outlets to charge our vehicles, so I can do that.  My commute is about 14 miles. That's 3-4 hours of charging at the slowest rate possible. If people have access to slow charging at home and/or at work, then most people will rarely need a supercharger. Even if I had a commute of, say 50 miles (which I used to have), if I had a Chevy Bolt with 200 miles of range and a level 2 charger, I could charge up that 100 miles pretty much every night. That's about 10-12 hours of charging per day.
Having that access if you own your home is pretty easy and cheap. A portable level 1 adapter is about 150-200 dollars. My garage has an outlet. I made sure I my breaker was at least a 20 amps and installed a newer plug with a built in surge protector for 30 bucks. 
I went from going to the gas station about once per week to almost never going to a gas station. We don't need there to be a billion charging stations if people can charge at home. Which is the real problem.
If I lived in an apartment, I wouldn't be so lucky. We need to incentivize retro fitting already existing housing to include access to level 1 charging (which just needs access to a standard 110 plug and that plug). If you make it economical to do this, it will happen. I live in an area where the vast majority of people think global warming is a hoax, but at least a quarter of the houses in my neighborhood have solar panels because the tax incentives made them a smart economic choice. It was cheaper to install solar than it was to buy electricity from the grid for about 5 years. Since that program ended, the amount of solar systems installed has dropped dramatically. There is no way that apartment landlords will do this unless it makes them money, so provide incentives that will do that.
Anyway,  installing those in parking spaces would be moderately expensive but far less expensive than creating the supercharging infrastructure we're talking about here. New buildings should be required or at least incentivized to have access to at least a 110 plug in their parking spaces. With home access to charging, the vast majority of EV owners will rarely have to visit a charging station and we don't have to worry about creating this massive infrastructure and with home access to charging the access to charging issue basically disappears.

The issue is access to home charging. I own a Chevy Volt and I own my house. I charge it up at night and I never have to visit a charging station. The Volt also has an ICE engine in it as well, so while my range on electric is about 48 miles, I have as much actual range as I want. However, I almost never use the ICE engine. It turns on when it is really cold outside to heat up the battery and if I go on a trip over 50 miles, I'll use it, but I'd say 90% of my miles are electric. Still, it only has that 48 miles of electric power and I almost never go over that because I can charge it at home. If I can drive almost entirely electric on 48 miles of range, then most people could do it with 200-300. I don't even have a fast charger. I only have a Level 1 charger, which gets me about 4 miles per hour of charge, but I generally don't drive more than 40 miles per day, so it doesn't matter. My work also lets us plug into 110 outlets to charge our vehicles, so I can do that. My commute is about 14 miles. That's 3-4 hours of charging at the slowest rate possible. If people have access to slow charging at home and/or at work, then most people will rarely need a supercharger. Even if I had a commute of, say 50 miles (which I used to have), if I had a Chevy Bolt with 200 miles of range and a level 2 charger, I could charge up that 100 miles pretty much every night. That's about 10-12 hours of charging per day. Having that access if you own your home is pretty easy and cheap. A portable level 1 adapter is about 150-200 dollars. My garage has an outlet. I made sure I my breaker was at least a 20 amps and installed a newer plug with a built in surge protector for 30 bucks. I went from going to the gas station about once per week to almost never going to a gas station. We don't need there to be a billion charging stations if people can charge at home. Which is the real problem. If I lived in an apartment, I wouldn't be so lucky. We need to incentivize retro fitting already existing housing to include access to level 1 charging (which just needs access to a standard 110 plug and that plug). If you make it economical to do this, it will happen. I live in an area where the vast majority of people think global warming is a hoax, but at least a quarter of the houses in my neighborhood have solar panels because the tax incentives made them a smart economic choice. It was cheaper to install solar than it was to buy electricity from the grid for about 5 years. Since that program ended, the amount of solar systems installed has dropped dramatically. There is no way that apartment landlords will do this unless it makes them money, so provide incentives that will do that. Anyway, installing those in parking spaces would be moderately expensive but far less expensive than creating the supercharging infrastructure we're talking about here. New buildings should be required or at least incentivized to have access to at least a 110 plug in their parking spaces. With home access to charging, the vast majority of EV owners will rarely have to visit a charging station and we don't have to worry about creating this massive infrastructure and with home access to charging the access to charging issue basically disappears.

Author: @julianruggiero9701
Text: The good news is, the US has pretty much settled on CCS being the standard for DC fast charging. All the new non-Tesla EVs coming out use CCS, and Tesla recently released a CCS adapter for their cars. So standardization is fine now. We just need more charging stations, and we need those stations to be online and operational. Currently, Electrify America owns the largest non-Tesla DC fast charging network in the US. Their station reliability is terrible. I hear horror stories every day from people that drove to a 4-stall charging station, only to find all 4 chargers were out of order. Their only option was to call for a tow truck. So we still have an infrastructure problem, it's just now it's shifted to being a reliability problem more than anything else.

Author: @SourDonut99
Text:
North America use CCS1 which is just J1772 with two additional prongs on the bottom. 

Something like a Chevy bolt can charge from both J1772 and CCS1.

Tesla switched to J1772 so they can do the same. 

But rip to CHADEMO which is on a sinking ship. You can find some chargers with that standard but RIP if your car uses that because there's not many. 

Europe uses CCS2 which has the same bottom two prongs as CCS1 but the top prongs are different. Mainly because NA and Japan uses 110/120v while Europe uses 220/240v. This will never change and our cars will never be compatible due to power grid differences. That's largely fine

So NA does have a standard. It's J1772 and CCS1 which is just the fast charging addition to J1772

Author: @jbill190
Text:
Every person who will point out all the problems with this is right, unfortunately. The batteries are too expensive, too heavy, too internal, and too specialized to just swap out quickly and conveniently. A number of years ago when EVs weren't really as much of a thing I thought about a station that would have spot you would drive over and stop your car.  A specialized articulated arm would grab, unlock, and then remove the standard shielded battery from the bottom of your car and then slide it away to an available charging slot (also below ground level) while another arm already carrying a standard battery that had been charged to full would move into position underneath your car, install the battery, and reengage the lock. And then you would drive away. The whole process could take less time than filling a tank of gas, and since it's automated (it would absolutely have to be) payment could also be automated. The whole thing could be done in less than 30 seconds and you wouldn't even have to get out of your car.

It would require some things that are difficult for a number of reasons:
- Robot arms are expensive. And anything built under ground level is expensive. Even if you make an installation that only has 1 of these mechanisms the cost is probably still more than 50 DC fast chargers. Not to mention dangerous if someone had a part of their body under the car when batteries were being swapped. Not to mention a nightmare to maintain.
- Companies would have an extremely hard time standardizing these batteries. This actually might be the most realistic aspect of my little dream, but making a battery that is tough enough to be installed in a vehicle's undercarriage, and large enough to provide decent range (it wouldn't have to be as good range if this whole idea ever really got off the ground since getting a charged battery would be so easy, but it would still have to be at least 100 miles range).
- A bunch of other little things I probably haven't thought of. Honestly, this might be a better idea for a work of speculative fiction. It's probably unworkable in the real world.

Author: @Swampster70
Cleaned_Text: @NixViridian Wyoming, as expected, is a little sparse when it comes to chargers but from a quick look at Chargehub, which lists most of the chargers out there - but not all, there are chargers at Casper and at Cheyenne. Typically, where you have a town that's at a junction of a freeway and a highway there'll be chargers - that's typically how things start. There are chargers at Douglas, Wheatland and Laramie between Casper and Cheyenne and several towns north of Casper have chargers too - I'm guessing you like north of Casper as it's closer to you than Cheyenne. A lot of vehicles now can do more than 250+ miles, so that 140 miles round trip to Casper wouldn't be an issue. Do the trip, come home, put it on a L2 charger and 4 hours later and you're back at full charge. 218 miles, if you have something like a Chevy Bolt would be doable, as long as you didn't cruise at 90mph all the way. The 280ish mile range may be reduced by excessive speed enough to make that 218 miles a bit on the tight side. at 80, it shouldn't be that bad - but there are chargers. Chargers tend not to be as visible as gas stations and tend to be closer to where other amenities are. Maybe when chargers go beyond the current 350kW that some can now achieve and charging times are below 10 minutes, this will change. Gas stations had to be visible because old cars didn't have GPS and touch screens to guide them. Now cars like Tesla's guide you to the best charging station and even precondition the battery for best charging time and to reduce wear on the battery.

Author: @Swampster70
Cleaned_Text: @NixViridian I don't think that prices of electricity will increase much specifically due to EV charging. As it is, a typical two stage HVAC unit pulls more power from the grid when on the second stage than a L2 (33 miles of range per hour charge) car charger would do at home. You don't even need to buy a L2 charger that connects to a 240V outlet if your typical car usage is under 50 miles a day. A lot of the time I'll connect my standard 110V charger to my car and essentially trickle charge it overnight at a whopping 4 miles of charge per hour. Here we get cheap rate from 11pm at night until 3pm the following afternoon. That 110V charger pulls far less power than appliances such as a hairdryer or a kettle. Mine pulls 11.5A - so less than the 1,300 watts the wife's hairdryer pulls. Even if you only do 30 to 50 miles per day and then every once in a while you go to Casper or Cheyenne you could make sure you're topped off before you go and maybe grab a charger on your way back to reduce the at-home charging time when you return.

Author: @peterjorgensen3
Text:
A few things - A lot has changed in the years since this video. Like the Ioniq 5 and EV6.

On trips EVs don't charge to 100% usually. Often 65% is all that's needed, sometimes 80%. The whole 100% by 31 minutes isn't really valid or useful. Better to look at getting 300 miles of range by 31 minutes. That may mean the total range is 400 miles but you're not using all of it.

Tesla and many others precondition batteries so they charge just as fast in cold weather. This also means there isn't really any range loss in colder weather and no charging speed loss.

Author: @marviwilson1853
Text: I wonder why charging stations don't incorporate large batteries as part of their make up. When a car comes for DC charging it gets its energy from the battery. This battery is then constantly charged from the AC grid using a smaller and cheaper AC-DC converter, (Rectifier!!) or else less of the bigger expensive one's. In some respects then the battery is like the old gas station main gas tank and the battery charging is like a continuous fuel delivery lorry. Clearly a balancing act between energy in from the grid and energy out from the DC chargers but their maybe many scenarios where this would be better.

Author: @davidburke4101
Text: I would assume because the cost is most likely higher than just using a converter. The battery cannot just be the same size as the vehicle. It would probably have to be 5-10x larger than the vehicles battery, due to conversion losses and needing to charge many cars in a row. That cost eventually adds up to the point where the converter is at cost parity or cheaper. And batteries use expensive limited supply materials. Even if it is slightly cheaper to produce a battery based charging station, that material could instead be used to make a battery to sell another electric car, or to industrial purposes which will generate far more return.
